original_hypothesis.title: Guard Bypass via Non-Standard NFT Function Selectors Not Covered by _checkTransaction
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Guard._checkTransaction only blocks a hardcoded set of ERC721/ERC1155 function selectors (transferFrom, safeTransferFrom, approve, setApprovalForAll, safeBatchTransferFrom). Any non-standard transfer or approval function (e.g., EIP-4494 permit, custom burn, permit-and-transfer patterns) is NOT blocked. If a rented NFT implements additional transfer mechanisms beyond the standard selectors, the renter can use these to move or approve rented assets out of the safe. The guard's else branch for unknown selectors does nothing except check the three specific known-bad selectors.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The _checkTransaction function uses an if/else if chain for specific selectors. Any selector not in this chain falls through to the final else block which only checks three hardcoded selectors. All other selectors are implicitly ALLOWED.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Hardcoded selector checks only cover 5 ERC721 and 2 ERC1155 selectors plus 3 Safe selectors. All other selectors pass through without blocking.

preconditions: Rented NFT implements a transfer or approval mechanism with a function selector not in the guard's hardcoded list
Active rental exists with such an NFT in the safe
No hook is active for the NFT contract (or hook doesn't block the selector either)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Guard only checks ~10 selectors; ERC721/ERC1155 contracts can implement arbitrary additional functions
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Renter calls permit(spender, tokenId, deadline, sig) or custom transferNFT() on the rented asset; guard does not block
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full theft of rented NFT via non-standard transfer/approval mechanism

economic_questions: Which popular NFT collections implement EIP-4494 permit or other non-standard transfer mechanisms?
What percentage of rentable NFTs have non-standard transfer functions?
Can burn() functions be used to destroy rented assets?
validation_requirements: Deploy an NFT with EIP-4494 permit, rent it, call permit from safe, verify guard allows it
Enumerate common non-standard NFT selectors and test against guard

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of fulfilling a rental order for a target NFT
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of the rented NFT

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Lender of NFTs with non-standard transfer mechanisms
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rentals involving NFTs with non-standard selectors

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Catalog all non-standard selectors in top NFT collections
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm unmatched selectors pass through guard without blocking

validation_result.impact_summary: Guard's _checkTransaction only blocks ~10 hardcoded selectors. Any non-standard transfer/approval function (e.g., EIP-4494 permit) bypasses the guard, enabling theft of rented NFTs that implement such functions.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The Guard._checkTransaction function uses an if/else if chain to check a fixed set of ERC721/ERC1155 function selectors. The final else block only checks 3 additional selectors (setApprovalForAll, safeBatchTransferFrom, setGuard). Any function selector NOT in this hardcoded set is implicitly ALLOWED to execute. If a rented NFT implements a non-standard approval mechanism like EIP-4494 permit(address,uint256,uint256,bytes), the renter can call it from the safe to grant an approval to an external address. The guard does not recognize the permit selector and allows the transaction. Once the approval is granted, the approved address can call transferFrom on the NFT contract directly (not from the safe, so the guard is not involved) to transfer the rented NFT out of the safe. This constitutes a complete bypass of the guard's protection for any NFT with non-standard transfer or approval mechanisms.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Guard.sol _checkTransaction function contains a finite set of blocked selectors. The else branch at the end of the if/else chain only checks 3 selectors (shared_set_approval_for_all_selector, e1155_safe_batch_transfer_from_selector, gnosis_safe_set_guard_selector). All other selectors fall through without any revert, meaning they are implicitly allowed. This is a blocklist approach that cannot protect against unknown function selectors.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The else branch at the end of the if/else chain only blocks 3 specific selectors. Any other selector (e.g., EIP-4494 permit, custom burn, non-standard transfer functions) falls through without any revert, allowing execution from the rental safe. This enables granting approvals via non-standard mechanisms that the guard does not recognize.

calling_sequence: 1. Renter initiates a transaction from the rental safe calling permit(spender, tokenId, deadline, sig) on a rented EIP-4494 NFT
2. Safe calls Guard.checkTransaction with to=NFT_address, data=permit(...)
3. Guard checks operation != DelegateCall (passes - this is a Call)
5. Guard checks for hook on NFT address (assume no hook configured)
8. Selector does not match any of the if/else if branches (not transferFrom, not safeTransferFrom, not approve, not ERC1155 selectors, not module selectors)
9. Falls to else branch: selector != setApprovalForAll, != safeBatchTransferFrom, != setGuard
10. Function returns without reverting - permit call is ALLOWED
11. NFT's permit function grants approval to spender
12. Spender calls NFT.transferFrom(safe, spender, tokenId) externally - guard not involved
13. Rented NFT is stolen from the safe
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Guard.checkTransaction -> no hook -> _checkTransaction -> selector not in hardcoded list -> falls through else branch -> 3 additional checks don't match -> function returns without revert -> transaction allowed

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts directly relevant to the guard's selector list. The guard's selector list is hardcoded in the contract and cannot be configured at deployment.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is valid: (1) Lender lists an NFT that implements EIP-4494 permit for rent. (2) Renter creates rental, NFT goes to rental safe. (3) Renter initiates a safe transaction calling permit(attackerAddress, rentedTokenId, deadline, signature) on the NFT. (4) Guard.checkTransaction is called - the permit selector is not in the hardcoded blocklist, so the transaction is allowed. (5) The permit grants approval to attackerAddress. (6) AttackerAddress calls NFT.transferFrom(safe, attacker, tokenId) directly on the NFT contract (not through the safe), bypassing the guard entirely. (7) The rented NFT is now in the attacker's possession. The precondition is that the rented NFT implements a non-standard approval/transfer mechanism with a selector not in the guard's blocklist.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Any rented NFT that implements additional transfer or approval mechanisms beyond the standard ERC721/ERC1155 selectors can be stolen from the rental safe. This includes NFTs implementing EIP-4494 (permit), custom transfer functions, or any other approval-granting mechanism. The impact is total loss of the lender's NFT. While the precondition requires a non-standard NFT, EIP-4494 adoption is growing and the guard provides a false sense of security.
recommended_mitigations: Implement checkAfterExecution to verify that all rented assets are still in the safe after every transaction, reverting if any are missing
Consider an allowlist approach: only allow known-safe function selectors to be called on addresses holding rented assets, rather than trying to block all dangerous selectors
Add a general check that if the target address (to) holds any rented assets (STORE.isRentedOut), restrict the transaction to only explicitly allowed selectors
For NFT contract addresses with active rentals, default to blocking all unknown selectors rather than allowing them
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy an ERC721 token that implements EIP-4494 permit (or a custom approval function with a non-standard selector). 2. Create a rental order with this NFT as the rented asset. 3. From the rental safe, call the permit function on the NFT to approve an external address. 4. Verify the Guard allows the transaction (does not revert). 5. From the approved external address, call transferFrom to move the NFT out of the safe. 6. Verify the NFT has been transferred out despite being actively rented.
uncertainties: The practical prevalence of EIP-4494 or other non-standard transfer mechanisms in popular NFT collections is uncertain
The protocol may intend to only support standard ERC721/ERC1155 tokens, though this is not enforced at rental creation time
Custom hooks could potentially be configured per-NFT-contract to cover non-standard selectors, but this requires admin action for each unique NFT
additional_testing_needed: Survey popular NFT collections for non-standard transfer/approval functions
Test with NFTs that have custom burn() functions to verify asset destruction is possible
Test with NFTs that have increaseAllowance-style functions
Test whether any popular NFTs have functions that can modify approval state through selectors not in the guard's blocklist
